title: The Solar System

sources.label: NASA Science: Solar System Family Portrait

review_notes: Explain that many solar-system visuals are models or composites, not single-camera photos of the entire system.
Keep NASA image credits visible when adapting the deck.
Distances and planet sizes are simplified for Year 5.

slides.title: The Solar System
slides.subtitle: A tour of the Sun, planets, and smaller worlds that share our cosmic neighbourhood.

slides.title: Understand how the Solar System is organised

cards.title: Name
cards.text: Identify the Sun and the eight planets.

cards.title: Order
cards.text: Describe where planets sit from inner to outer.

cards.title: Explain
cards.text: Use gravity and orbit to explain the system.

slides.callout: Learning intention: explain why the Sun is central and how planets move around it.

slides.title: A system held together by gravity
slides.lead: The Solar System includes the Sun and the objects that orbit it: planets, dwarf planets, moons, asteroids, comets, and smaller pieces of space material.

bullets.title: Center
bullets.text: The Sun is the star at the center.

bullets.title: Motion
bullets.text: Planets travel around the Sun in orbits.

bullets.title: Scale
bullets.text: Distances are huge, so classroom diagrams are usually not to scale.

slides.source_note: Sources: NASA Science Solar System and Planets pages.

slides.title: One image can show the family, but not true scale
slides.lead: Official NASA visuals help us inspect the planets, but many Solar System overview images are models or composites because the system is too large for one ordinary photo.
slides.caption: NASA Science family portrait visual.
slides.source_note: Source: NASA Science Solar System Family Portrait.

slides.title: Star, planets, smaller worlds

cards.title: The Sun
cards.text: A star that gives light and heat and contains most of the Solar System’s mass.

cards.title: The planets
cards.text: Eight major worlds orbit the Sun in a regular order.

cards.title: Smaller bodies
cards.text: Moons, asteroids, comets, and dwarf planets are part of the system too.

slides.note: Teacher note: keep Pluto as a dwarf planet, not one of the eight planets.
slides.source_note: Sources: NASA Science Solar System and Planets pages.

slides.title: Everything connects back to the Sun
slides.lead: Gravity keeps planets and smaller worlds moving around the Sun instead of drifting away in straight lines.

slides.title: Eight planets orbit the Sun
slides.lead: A simple order helps us talk about inner and outer planets.

items.title: Dwarf planets
items.text: Pluto and others are not counted among the eight planets.

slides.title: Classroom diagrams usually lie about scale
slides.lead: If we draw the planets large enough to see, the distances cannot fit on the same slide. If we draw distances accurately, planets become too tiny to inspect.

cards.title: Size problem
cards.text: Planets are much smaller than the spaces between them.

cards.title: Distance problem
cards.text: The outer planets are far beyond the inner planets.

slides.callout: Teacher move: ask students what the model helps us see and what it hides.
slides.source_note: Source-grounded simplification from NASA Solar System overview.

slides.title: Build a human Solar System model

steps.label: Assign
steps.title: Give roles
steps.text: Students become the Sun, planets, asteroid belt, and a comet.

steps.label: Order
steps.title: Line up
steps.text: Place planets from Mercury to Neptune and check the order.

steps.label: Reflect
steps.title: Spot the limits
steps.text: Discuss which parts of the model are accurate and which are simplified.

slides.note: Optional extension: students estimate which planets need more space in a true scale model.

slides.title: Use real images with clear model language
slides.lead: NASA images are excellent for source-grounded visuals, but Solar System overview pictures often combine data, scale choices, and artistic layout.

cards.title: Say
cards.text: This is a NASA visual/model that helps us compare objects.

cards.title: Avoid
cards.text: Do not imply one camera photographed all planets together at true scale.

slides.callout: Source grounding status: grounded; classroom review pending.

slides.title: Show your understanding
prompts: One way diagrams can mislead us is...
slides.challenge: Challenge: explain why a model can be useful even when it is not perfectly accurate.

slides.title: Sources used
slides.note: Image policy: NASA/public source visuals with credits; no AI-generated planet photos.
